texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We present an expectation-maximization (EM) based unified framework for nonnegative tensor decomposition that optimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence. To avoid iterations in each M-step and learning rate tuning, we establish a general relationship between low-rank decompositions and many-body approximations. Using this connection, we exploit that the closed-form solution of the many-body approximation updates all parameters simultaneously in the M-step. Our framework offers not only a unified methodology for a variety of low-rank structures, including CP, Tucker, and Tensor Train decompositions, but also their mixtures. Notably, the weights of each low-rank tensor in the mixture can be learned from the data, which enables us to leverage the advantage of different low-rank structures without careful selection of the structure in advance. We empirically demonstrate that our framework overall provides superior generalization in terms of discrete density estimation and classification when compared to conventional tensor-based approaches.
texts.text: We present an expectation-maximization (EM) based unified framework for nonnegative tensor decomposition that optimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence. To avoid iterations in each M-step and learning rate tuning, we establish a general relationship between low-rank decompositions and many-body approximations. Using this connection, we exploit that the closed-form solution of the many-body approximation updates all parameters simultaneously in the M-step. Our framework offers not only a unified methodology for a variety of low-rank structures, including CP, Tucker, and Tensor Train decompositions, but also their mixtures. Notably, the weights of each low-rank tensor in the mixture can be learned from the data, which enables us to leverage the advantage of different low-rank structures without careful selection of the structure in advance. We empirically demonstrate that our framework overall provides superior generalization in terms of discrete density estimation and classification when compared to conventional tensor-based approaches.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Tensors are versatile data structures used in a broad range of fields including signal processing [55], computer vision [46], and data mining [47]. It is an established fact that features can be extracted from tensor-formatted data by low-rank decomposition, which approximates the tensor by a linear combination of a few bases [10, 37]. There are numerous variations of low-rank structure used in the decomposed representation, such as CP [23, 5], Tucker [61], and Tensor Train (TT) [45], and the user needs to select one structure in advance before performing the decomposition.

It is an established fact that features can be extracted from tensor-formatted data by low-rank decomposition, which approximates the tensor by a linear combination of a few bases [10, 37]. There are numerous variations of low-rank structure used in the decomposed representation, such as CP [23, 5], Tucker [61], and Tensor Train (TT) [45], and the user needs to select one structure in advance before performing the decomposition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A series of recent studies [21, 42] show that tensor low-rank decomposition is also useful for discrete density estimation, which is an interesting application that takes advantage of the discreteness of the tensor indices. Specifically, given observed discrete samples x (1) , . . . , x ( N ) , the empirical distribution p ( x ) can be regarded as a non-negative normalized tensor T , called an empirical tensor , and its low-rank reconstruction P approximates the true distribution as seen in Figure 1. The obtained density can then be used for multiple purposes, such as predicting new data points, classification, inferring missing values, or performing outlier detection [54]; however, two challenges remain in these current works.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address these two challenges, we proposes a unified non-negative tensor factorization method based on the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm [12]. The EM algorithm is an iterative framework for maximum likelihood estimation that repeatedly maximizes the lower bound of the log-likelihood function in two steps, the E-step and the M-step. The naive EM-based formulation for general non-negative tensor decomposition involves either alternating optimization to bound the log-likelihood (E-step) and maximizing each factor individually (M-step), or using gradient-based methods in each M-step. The former approach requires tailoring methods for each type of low-rank structure, while the latter is computationally expensive because of the additional iteration for the gradient method inside the EM iteration. To overcome this issue, we exploit that the optimization in each M-step coincides with a tensor many-body approximation [19] that decomposes tensors by a representation with reduced interactions among tensor modes. We derive the exact closedform solution for many-body approximation that appears in the M-step for the Tucker and TT decompositions, and thereby successfully remove the gradient method in the M-step for various kinds of low-rank decomposition by combining these two formulas.
texts.text: To address these two challenges, we proposes a unified non-negative tensor factorization method based on the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm [12]. The EM algorithm is an iterative framework for maximum likelihood estimation that repeatedly maximizes the lower bound of the log-likelihood function in two steps, the E-step and the M-step. The naive EM-based formulation for general non-negative tensor decomposition involves either alternating optimization to bound the log-likelihood (E-step) and maximizing each factor individually (M-step), or using gradient-based methods in each M-step. The former approach requires tailoring methods for each type of low-rank structure, while the latter is computationally expensive because of the additional iteration for the gradient method inside the EM iteration. To overcome this issue, we exploit that the optimization in each M-step coincides with a tensor many-body approximation [19] that decomposes tensors by a representation with reduced interactions among tensor modes. We derive the exact closedform solution for many-body approximation that appears in the M-step for the Tucker and TT decompositions, and thereby successfully remove the gradient method in the M-step for various kinds of low-rank decomposition by combining these two formulas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our framework inherits the properties of the EM algorithm [27], and therefore, it always converges regardless of the choice of the low-rank structure assumed in the model. Furthermore, the tensor to be approximated in the M-step is sparse as it is defined in terms of elementwise multiplications by the empirical tensor. As a result, the computational complexity of the proposed method is proportional to the number of samples N . Notably, as the EM algorithm is frequently used for maximum likelihood estimation of mixture models, the proposed method allows for density estimation with mixtures of low-rank tensors providing flexible modeling. The mixture model automatically finds appropriate weights for mixed low-rank structures, eliminating the need for the user to define a single low-rank structure in advance, which allows hybridization of the merits of each low-rank structure, i.e., the direct representation in CP and the latent interaction in TT.

texts.orig: Moreover, the flexibility allows us to mix a low-rank tensor with a constant tensor, which incorporates the noise of the data and stabilizes learning. While adding a small uniform constant to a tensor is often used as a heuristic to stabilize learning [11, 20], our method provides a principled approach to learning the constant from the data. We empirically show that such mixture of low-rank modeling provides better generalization in classification and density estimation tasks than pure low-rank tensor models. We summarize our contribution as follows:

texts.orig: Problem setup We construct a normalized empirical tensor , or empirical distribution, from the given tabular data set with D categorical features. The distribution is identical to a normalized D -th order tensor T ∈ R I 1 ×···× I D ≥ 0 where I d is the degree of freedom (i.e., categories) of the d -th categorical feature. The number of non-zero values in the tensor is equivalent to the number of observed samples N if there is no identical sample in the data. To estimate the discrete probability distribution underlying the data, we approximate the tensor T with a low-rank tensor considering the CP, Tucker, and TT formats, or their mixture as a convex linear combination of low-rank tensors. The setup for D = 3 is shown in Figure 1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Related works
texts.text: 2 Related works

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The EM algorithm is widely used to train models with hidden variables [12]. We apply the EM algorithm to tensor decomposition by regarding tensor indices as visible variables and ranks as hidden variables. Several studies have shown that decomposing an empirical tensor constructed from observed samples can be used to estimate the underlying distribution behind the data [29, 21, 26, 62]. For density estimation, the KL divergence is a natural choice for the objective function of tensor decomposition, and the multiplicative updates methods (MU) are here often used to find the low-rank tensor by optimizing the KL divergence from an empirical tensor [31, 49]. The present EM-based method has not been established except for the CP decomposition [24, 65, 6]. Importantly, our work provides EM-based decomposition for various low-rank structures and their mixtures and enables the update of all parameters simultaneously, which differs from the MU methods that alternatingly update a specific set of parameters while keeping the other parameters fixed. Although a mixture model with the same low-rank structures is considered in [63], our approach incorporates a mixture of different low-rank structures, which is a more general framework. Recently, density estimation methods using second and third-order marginals have been developed [30, 26, 22]. However, these methods are so far limited to considering the CP decomposition-based model and typically require hyper-parameters for the gradient method, whereas the convergence of the algorithms has not been fully discussed. In this context, our approach directly applies to various low-rank structures with hyper-parameter-free optimization and guarantees monotonically decreasing error functions and convergence.

texts.orig: In probabilistic tensor decomposition, tensor elements are sampled from a distribution p θ and the model parameter θ is optimized via the EM algorithm [32, 66, 51]. We note that our setting is different from these works as we do not assume any distribution behind each element of the tensor. Furthermore, the EM algorithm is often used for tensor completion, treating missing values as hidden variables [60, 39, 56]. This task assumes T i = P i where T is the given tensor including missing values, P is the reconstructed low-rank tensor, and i is an index on observed elements of T . Density estimation does not impose this constraint. It has also been reported that the EM algorithm can be applied to sum-product networks [13] by regarding sum-nodes as hidden variables [48]. texts.orig: 4 Density estimation via tensor learning
texts.text: 4 Density estimation via tensor learning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the exact solutions of the many-body approximation derived in Section 3, we develop a novel framework EM non-negative tensor learning for discrete density estimation. Our framework has two advantages: (1) it achieves linear computational complexity relative to the number of nonzero elements in the input tensor, as described in Section 4.2; and (2) it offers the flexibility to incorporate various low-rank structures, such as CP, Tucker, TT, their mixtures, and adaptive noise terms, while preserving the convexity of E-step and M-step, as detailed in Sections 4.3. Neither of these advantages has been explored in MU methods [31]. In the following, tensor indices for mixtures are expressed as superscripts without parentheses.
texts.text: Based on the exact solutions of the many-body approximation derived in Section 3, we develop a novel framework EM non-negative tensor learning for discrete density estimation. texts.orig: While the EM-based method for CP decomposition optimizing the KL divergence has already been developed in [24, 65], our proposed approach addresses more general low-rank decompositions and their mixtures. We provide the entire algorithm in Algorithm 1. Notably, the framework does not require a learning rate that needs to be carefully tuned when relying on gradient-based methods. Moreover, the proposed method updates all parameters simultaneously in closed form, which differs from the multiplicative update rule such as [31]. The extension to non-normalized non-negative tensors is described in Section C.2 of the supplemental material.

texts.orig: Adaptive noise learning Our approach deals not only with typical low-rank structures and their mixtures but also with noise terms. For example, we here define model P as the mixture of a low-rank tensor P low-rank and a normalized uniform tensor P noise whose elements all are 1 / | Ω I | as P = (1 -η noise ) P low-rank + η noise P noise , for | Ω I | = I 1 I 2 . . . I D . The learnable parameter η noise indicates the magnitude of global shift or background noise in the data. It is obvious from the above discussion that the convergence remains even in the presence of the noise term. We show in Section 5 that the noise term also stabilizes the learning.

I D . The learnable parameter η noise indicates the magnitude of global shift or background noise in the data. It is obvious from the above discussion that the convergence remains even in the presence of the noise term. We show in Section 5 that the noise term also stabilizes the learning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selection of mixture components As shown above, our framework supports the mixtures of lowrank tensors. This flexibility raises the question of how to determine the components of the mixture. One approach is to manually select the low-rank structures to leverage the strengths of different structures to improve generalization in density estimation. For instance, the CP decomposition offers an intuitive representation with fewer parameters; the TT decomposition captures latent pairwise interactions using more parameters; while the Tucker decomposition accounts for all combinations of latent interactions across the attributes, resulting in an exponential increase in the number of parameters. Alternatively, we can employ cross-validation to automatically select suitable structures. We also emphasize that it is not always possible to uniquely define the optimal mixture components

While the proposed framework can explore a variety of low-rank structures and their mixtures, we here focus on the performance of EMCP, EMTrain, and their mixture EMCPTT, both with and without adaptive noise terms, due to their scalable properties. In all of the following experiments, the discrete samples are divided into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test samples to form training, validation, and test tensors, respectively. Tensor ranks are tuned so that the low-rank tensor reconstructed from the training tensor by the proposed or baseline method best approximates the validation tensor. The setup is more detailed in Section E in the supplementary material.

texts.orig: Effect of the adaptive noise term In Figure 5(bottom), we show the validation errors for CarEvaluation, obtained with and without the adaptive noise term in the proposed method, with varying tensor ranks. The validation negative log-likelihood often becomes large when the model has no adaptive noise term caused by numerical instability due to extremely small values in the logarithm function. The noise term eliminates this problem and prevents the rapid increase in validation error when the number of model parameters is large stabilizing the learning. We observe similar results for other datasets in Figures 7 in the Appendix.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Conclusion
texts.text: 6 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While it is a well-established principle that both real- and complex-valued tensors can be approximated with various low-rank structures based on local singular value decomposition (SVD), a unified framework for non-negative low-rank approximation has not been developed. Consequently, nonnegative tensor factorizations typically require piecemeal tailored implementations or gradient methods. This study introduces an EM-based unified framework that decouples the non-negative low-rank structure into multiple solvable many-body approximations and applies a closed-form solution locally, thereby eliminating the need for gradient methods. Our framework not only uniformly handles various low-rank structures but also supports adaptive noise terms and mixtures of low-rank tensors without losing the convergence guarantee. The flexibility of our modeling stems from the generality of the EM algorithm and a formulation that can take advantage of the logarithmic function properties that appear in the KL divergence. Empirical results demonstrate that our framework overall achieves superior generalization performance in classification and density estimation tasks compared to the considered baseline methods.
texts.text: While it is a well-established principle that both real- and complex-valued tensors can be approximated with various low-rank structures based on local singular value decomposition (SVD), a unified framework for non-negative low-rank approximation has not been developed. Consequently, nonnegative tensor factorizations typically require piecemeal tailored implementations or gradient methods. This study introduces an EM-based unified framework that decouples the non-negative low-rank structure into multiple solvable many-body approximations and applies a closed-form solution locally, thereby eliminating the need for gradient methods. Our framework not only uniformly handles various low-rank structures but also supports adaptive noise terms and mixtures of low-rank tensors without losing the convergence guarantee. The flexibility of our modeling stems from the generality of the EM algorithm and a formulation that can take advantage of the logarithmic function properties that appear in the KL divergence. Empirical results demonstrate that our framework overall achieves superior generalization performance in classification and density estimation tasks compared to the considered baseline methods.

texts.orig: In the following, we provide proofs of the closed-form solutions of many-body approximation in Figure 3( b ) and ( c ). When a factor in a low-body tensor is multiplied by ν , the value of the objective function of many-body approximation remains the same if another factor is multiplied by 1 /ν , which we call the scaling redundancy . The key idea in the following proofs is reducing the scaling redundancy and absorbing the normalizing conditions of the entire tensor into a single factor. This enables the decoupling of the normalized condition of the entire tensor into independent conditions about factors. This trick is also used in Section C.1 to decouple more complicated low-rank structures into a combination of CP, Tucker, and Train decompositions.

texts.orig: B Convergence Analysis
texts.text: B Convergence Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The convergence of the algorithm can be analyzed by examining the behavior of the objective function at the accumulation points, which are the convergence points of subsequences of the point sequence generated by the algorithm at each step. In several matrix factorization methods, it has been shown that, under certain assumptions, the accumulation points of the sequence generated by the algorithm are stationary points of the objective function [36, 58]. By a similar analysis, we show following two propositions related to the accumulation points of the point sequences generated by our proposed method.
texts.text: The convergence of the algorithm can be analyzed by examining the behavior of the objective function at the accumulation points, which are the convergence points of subsequences of the point sequence generated by the algorithm at each step. In several matrix factorization methods, it has been shown that, under certain assumptions, the accumulation points of the sequence generated by the algorithm are stationary points of the objective function [36, 58]. By a similar analysis, we show following two propositions related to the accumulation points of the point sequences generated by our proposed method.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The accumulation points are included in the domain of the objective function L and its lower bound L
texts.text: The accumulation points are included in the domain of the objective function L and its lower bound L

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The Lagrange function with the objective function and constraint for interactions is a stationary point at the accumulation points if all elements of the accumulation points have positive values.
texts.text: The Lagrange function with the objective function and constraint for interactions is a stationary point at the accumulation points if all elements of the accumulation points have positive values.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section is organized as follows. First, we explain in Section B.1 that we need to analyze the stationary points of the Lagrangian function, rather than the stationary points of the objective function directly, because the proposed method optimizes the objective function in the domain that satisfies constraints on nonnegativity and interactions among random variables, not in Euclidean space. Then, in Section B.2, we discuss the domain, continuity, and differentiability of the objective function and its lower bounds. In Section B.3, using the above discussion, we prove the compactness of the level set, which is assumed in typical analysis of EM algorithms, and demonstrate that the accumulation points are included in the domain of the objective function and its lower bound. Section B.4 proves that if all element of the tensor at an accumulation point are non-zero, then the Lagrange function is a stationary point at the accumulation points.

texts.orig: B.1.1 Conditional stationary point
texts.text: B.1.1 Conditional stationary point

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the standard discussion of EM algorithms, the probability distribution is parameterized by a parameter θ that takes real values freely, and convergence to a stationary point is guaranteed under certain assumptions by showing that the objective function L satisfies dL ( θ ∗ ) /dθ = 0 at the convergence point θ ∗ . In contrast, our approach directly optimizes the value of the probability distribution while restricting the interactions among random variables, rather than introducing such real valued parameters θ . Consequently, in the proposed method, the objective function L is optimized with respect to variables defined on a constrained space of interactions. This necessitates discussing convergence to a stationary point in terms of differentiation on the constrained space, rather than differentiation in a conventional Euclidean space.

texts.orig: Although Corollary 1 is mathematically correct, we admit that ˆ Q k,t ir can practically be treated as 0 if the value of ˆ Q k,t ir is very small positive value, due to the limit of the number of digits that the computer can handle. From the updates rule of the EM-algorithm, we can also confirm that once the value ˆ Q k,t becomes 0 , the value remains 0 throughout the algorithm. That is, ˆ Q k,T ir = 0 ⇒ ˆ Q k,t ir = 0 for any finite number t ≥ T , which is well-discussed phenomenon called inadmissible zeros [9]. Throughout the following discussion, however, we assume that the computer can deal with a sufficiently large number of digits, and consequently Corollary 1 is satisfied.

texts.orig: Since any bounded sequence of point converges to its set of accumulation points [52], the objective function of typical EM algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a stationary point under the assumptions of continuity for the cost function and its lower bound, as well as the compactness of the level set [27]. Although our proposed method satisfies all these assumptions as discussed so far, it should be noted that Theorem 5 does not demonstrate that every accumulation point is a stationary point of the objective function. Therefore, it is worth noting that even if all the elements in ˆ Q t are positive for some sufficiently large t , it is not guaranteed that the proposed algorithm has converged to a set of stationary points since we cannot exclude the possibility of oscillation between the boundary of the domain and the set of stationary points.

Therefore, it is worth noting that even if all the elements in ˆ Q t are positive for some sufficiently large t , it is not guaranteed that the proposed algorithm has converged to a set of stationary points since we cannot exclude the possibility of oscillation between the boundary of the domain and the set of stationary points.

texts.orig: C.2 EMtensor factorization for general non-negative tensors
texts.text: C.2 EMtensor factorization for general non-negative tensors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Non-negative tensor factorization optimizing the KL divergence is frequently used beyond density estimation and in various fields such as sound source separation [35], computer vision [31, 49], and data mining [9, 57, 33, 15]. Although the given tensor T is not necessarily normalized in such applications, the proposed framework can be used for them as follows. First, we obtain the total sum of the input tensor µ = ∑ i T i and then perform the factorization on the normalized tensor T by dividing all elements of T by µ . Finally, all elements of the resulting tensor P are multiplied by µ . This procedure is justified by the property of the KL divergence, D KL ( µ P , µ T ) = µD KL ( P , T ) , where µ is any positive value.

texts.orig: Tensors We refer to a tensor whose elements all are nonnegative as a nonnegative tensor. The axes of a tensor are called its modes. The number of modes is called the order. For example, a vector is a first-order tensor, and a matrix is a second-order tensor. Tensors are denoted by calligraphic capital letters, as T , P , Q , M . A non-negative tensor whose sum over all indices is 1 is called a normalized tensor. Although the beginning and the last core tensors of the TT format are matrices rather than tensors, they are denoted by the calligraphic letters G (1) and G ( D ) for notational convenience. The element-wise product of a normalized non-negative tensor Q k and a weight η k is written with a hat, e.g., ˆ Q k = η k Q k . In the supplementary materials, tensors and matrices whose sums over some indices equal 1 are marked with a tilde as ˜ Q .

texts.orig: C.4 Limitations
texts.text: C.4 Limitations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proposed framework only works on non-negative tensors. The theoretical analysis supporting the generalization performance of the mixture models remains in future work. While this study discussed low-rank structures with tree structures, such as CP, Tucker, and TT decomposition, and their combinations, we did not discuss tensor networks with loops. The method cannot be used directly in situations where there are missing values in the data. The method to decouple a complicated low-rank structure into solvable CP, Tucker, and TT decompositions is not unique. Thus, establishing an efficient decoupling method is also a subject for future work.

texts.orig: We download categorical tabular datasets Monk, SolarFlare, CarEvaluation, Nursery, SPECT, and Credit from the UCI database. 1 Since there are missing values in Credit, we used only the features with no missing values in the experiment. We also download two categorical tabular datasets, GermanGSS and Parity5p5, from Penn Machine Learning Benchmarks [44]. Each dataset contains N categorical samples x n = ( i 1 , ..., i D ) for n = 1 , . . . , N . Both the number of samples, N , and the sample dimension, D , vary across datasets as seen in Table 2. In the original datasets, each i d represents a categorical quantity such as color, location, gender, etc. By mapping these to natural numbers, each feature i d is converted to a natural number from 1 to I d , where I d is the degree of freedom in the d -th feature. We randomly select 70% of the N samples to create the training index set Ω train , 15% of samples to create the validation index set Ω valid , and the final 15% of the samples form the test index set Ω test . Some datasets may contain exactly the same samples. To deal with such datasets, we suppose that these indices sets may contain multiple identical elements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We download the categorical tabular datasets from UCI and Penn Machine Learning Benchmarks. We here describe the Data Set Selection Criteria. Since sparsity is a natural assumption in the density estimation, completely dense data were excluded. Furthermore, as too many classes with too sparse data are not well suited for the considered classifications, whereas the complexity of the proposed approach is proportional to N , we chose datasets whose sparseness is larger than 1.0e-5 and N < 20000 with fewer classes than 15 and with an empirical tensor of order larger than five.

texts.orig: The pseudocodes for proposed methods are described in Algorithms 1 and 2. All tensors and mixture ratios were initialized with a uniform distribution between 0 and 1 and normalized as necessary. The algorithm was terminated when the number of iterations of the EM step exceeded 1200 or when the difference of the log-likelihood from the previous iteration was below 10e-8. For each low-rank model, the rank was gradually increased until the number of parameters in the model was half the number of samples.

texts.orig: BMand KLTrain We downloaded the source code for Positive Matrix Product State (KLTrain) and Real Born Machine (BM) from the official repository. 2 The license of the code is described in the repository as MIT License. They optimize real-valued tensors and square each element to obtain nonnegative tensors. Each element of these real-valued tensors is initialized with the standard normal distribution. We varied the learning rates from 1.0e-4, 1.0e-3, ... to 1.0 for training data. We then used the learning rate that yielded the smallest validation score. According to the description in the README fi le, the batch size was fixed at 20 , and the number of iterations was set to 10,000. We performed each experiment five times with each bond and evaluated the mean and standard deviation. We varied the bond of the model as 1 , 2 , ..., 8 , and evaluated the test data with the bond that best fits the validation data.

texts.orig: CNMFOPT We implemented CNMFOPT according to the original paper [26]. We iteratively optimize the factor matrices A (1) , . . . , A ( D ) , and the weights λ one after the other. We use the exponentiated gradient method [4] to optimize each factor matrix and weight. Although this optimization can be performed by closed-form update rules, all parameters cannot be updated simultaneously.
texts.text: CNMFOPT We implemented CNMFOPT according to the original paper [26]. We iteratively optimize the factor matrices A (1) , . . . , A ( D ) , and the weights λ one after the other. We use the exponentiated gradient method [4] to optimize each factor matrix and weight. Although this optimization can be performed by closed-form update rules, all parameters cannot be updated simultaneously.

texts.orig: Thus, a loop is required for each update. This loop is called the inner iteration, which is repeated 100 times. The iterations of updating ( A (1) , . . . , A ( D ) , λ ) described above are called outer iteration. In the update rule for the exponential gradient method, the product of the derivative and the learning rate α is included in the exponential function. The learning rate α was selected from 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, and 0.00005 to minimize the validation error. Learning with a larger learning rate was not feasible because it caused an overflow of the exponential function. The initial values of the parameters follow a uniform random distribution. The algorithm terminates when one of the following conditions is met: (1) We compute the KL divergence from the input data to the reconstruction after updating all factor matrices and weights. The change is less than 1.0e-4 compared to the previous outer loop. (2) The number of outer iterations exceeds 600. When the condition (2) is met, the algorithm performs up to 60000( D +1) inner iterations in total.

texts.orig: Environment Experiments were conducted on Ubuntu 20.04.1 with a single core of 2.1GHz Intel Xeon CPU Gold 5218 and 128GB of memory. This work does not require GPU computing. The total computation time for all experiments, including tuning the learning rate of the baselines, was less than 320 hours, using 88 threads of parallel computing.
